title: Quantum Kernels for Parity-Structured Classification: A Hybrid Pipeline
abstract: Parity (XOR) classification requires detecting discrete, high-order feature interactions that smooth classical kernels cannot efficiently capture. We study how quantum kernel advantage depends on parity complexity, the number of features entering the XOR rule, and find a clear threshold behavior. We pair a ZZ quantum feature map with binary {0, pi} encoding (features median thresholded before circuit input) to expose parity structure. A binary encoding ablation, RBF SVM trained on the identical {0, pi} features, separates encoding from circuit effects: at low complexity (n = 5 features), binary RBF achieves 83.4% +/- 1.7% and the quantum kernel 81.2% +/- 1.9%, showing encoding drives performance there. At high complexity (n = 11 features, 11 qubits, r = 3 ZZ repetitions), all classical methods collapse to near-random (approx. 50%), binary RBF reaches only 54.3% +/- 1.1%, and the quantum ZZ kernel achieves 66.3% +/- 3.2% (mean +/- std, 10 seeds), a +12.0 percentage-point margin over the binary ablation and approx. 7x higher kernel-target alignment (0.094 +/- 0.020 vs. 0.013 +/- 0.001). These results identify parity complexity as a concrete axis along which genuine quantum kernel advantage, not attributable to encoding alone, emerges.
summary: This paper demonstrates that quantum kernels can outperform classical methods specifically when tackling high-order parity-structured classification tasks. It identifies a clear complexity threshold where the quantum ZZ feature map's ability to capture multi-feature correlations provides a genuine advantage over classical RBF kernels.
main_problem: Investigating whether quantum kernels provide a genuine advantage over classical kernels for high-order parity (XOR) classification tasks, and determining if this advantage stems from the quantum circuit's expressivity or merely the feature encoding.
main_result: A phase transition in performance was identified: for low parity complexity (n <= 8), classical binary encoding dominates, but for high complexity (n >= 9), the quantum ZZ kernel achieves a significant advantage, outperforming classical methods by up to 12 percentage points.
method: The study uses exact statevector simulations of a hybrid pipeline, comparing a ZZ quantum feature map against classical RBF SVMs and a 'binary encoding ablation' study to isolate circuit effects from encoding effects.
model_or_system: A quantum feature map using n qubits with a ZZ structure (comprising single-qucept rotations and entangling Z_i Z_j gates) applied to synthetic 500-dimensional data with n-way XOR parity labels.
key_observables: Test accuracy, Kernel-Target Alignment (KTA), and parity complexity (n).
important_parameters: Parity complexity (n = 5 to 11), number of ZZ repetitions (r = 3), and label noise (22%).
main_assumptions: The study assumes exact statevector simulation without hardware noise (shot noise, decoherence) and uses an 'oracle' feature selection method which may not reflect real-world feature discovery.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the comparison of Kernel-Target Alignment, highlighting the 7x superiority of the quantum ZZ kernel over RBF at n=11; Table I illustrates the performance transition/complexity sweep.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the parity classification problem, describes the quantum and classical models/encoding, details the simulation methodology, presents the complexity sweep results and ablation study, and concludes with a discussion on the source of quantum advantage and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Test accuracy (mean ± std over 10 seeds, n = 11, flip_y= 0.22). The quantum ZZ kernel substantially outperforms all classical baselines; all five continuous methods collapse to near-random, confirming that the advantage is structural rather than incidental.
Fig. 2. Kernel–target alignment comparison (n = 11). The quantum ZZ kernel shows ∼7× higher alignment with labels compared to RBF (0.094 vs. 0.013), consistent with the accuracy gap.
